reference: IEAGHG 2018-08 Table 8 — Nygaard 2010: dry CO2 (>95% purity) in supercritical state corrosion risk is LOW; not more severe than CO2-EOR operations

reference: IEAGHG 2018-08 §2.1.10: annual O&M including workover costs approximately double for CO2 EOR wells compared to conventional O&G wells

reference: IEAGHG 2018-08 §9 Summary (p.185) — Contek/API 2008: with modern material improvements and special tubing handling/installation techniques operators routinely expect tubular service life of 20-25 years in CO2 service
notes: Service life = planned maintenance lifecycle before replacement; distinct from MTTF (functional failure threshold); P10_MTTF=35yr captures 10th-percentile-worst early failures; 20-25yr planned life is consistent with P10 floor at 35yr only if failures are well-controlled — validates conservative tubing assumptions; older handling/installation practice produces shorter service life

reference: IEAGHG 2018-08 §2.1.8: corrosion logging of tubing every 4-5 years as directed by regulatory agency; Oxy Wasson MIT H-5 testing every 5 years (§8.4 — TRCC Rule §3.46 for all Class II injection wells)
notes: Standard monitoring tier detection_prob=0.40 for tubing reflects 4-5 year corrosion log cadence; comprehensive tier (0.60) reflects more frequent logging; minimal tier (0.20) reflects infrequent or absent caliper runs; regulatory baseline cadence corresponds to standard tier

reference: IEAGHG 2018-08 §8.4 Oxy Denver Unit Wasson Field — TRCC Rule §3.46: MIT H-5 testing every 5 years on all ~600 Class II injection wells; also required after any workover or casing repair; 24-hr notification to TRCC on failure; 60-day repair or plug deadline (40 CFR §98.230-238)
notes: 5-year MIT cadence validates detection_prob casing standard=0.30; Bradenhead gauge monitoring between MIT surveys provides interim SCP detection; idle wells >25yr also require MIT every 5 years (H-15 testing) with 30-day repair deadline; largest onshore CO2 EOR program in North America provides regulatory compliance baseline

reference: IEAGHG 2018-08 §8.6 Core Energy Michigan: 12 of 15 injection wells are converted (80%); no loss of well control incidents since CO2 EOR inception in 1996; no cement degradation observed; corrosion coupons show very little to no corrosion — dry CO2 without WAG is key enabling factor
notes: Dry CO2 without water contact is critical differentiator; legacy_conversion scenario 2.5× multiplier is appropriate for unknown construction history and inadequate pre-conversion assessment; properly assessed converted wells on dry CO2 can achieve near-new integrity; shallow casing issues unrelated to EOR zone have occurred and been remediated without major impact

reference: IEAGHG 2018-08 §3.1.2 / §2.1.6: MIT required by regulation after every workover that disturbs the tubing-packer-casing seal or casing; also required after squeeze cementing and re-completion operations before CO2 injection can resume; applies under TRCC §3.46 and 40 CFR §146
notes: Upgrades post_workover_verification_cost from best practice to documented regulatory obligation; validates cost as unavoidable per-workover expenditure rather than optional; MIT failure post-workover triggers same 60-day repair/plug deadline as routine 5-year testing; applies to all components disturbed during intervention

reference: IEAGHG 2018-08 §8.5 — Sminchak et al. 2013: systematic CBL analysis of 278 randomly sampled logs from Michigan (weighted avg 0.71) and Ohio (0.73); most wells had ≥15m cement rated >75% above isolation zone; SCP gas confirmed NOT from reservoir in 1940s wells
notes: Weighted average field cement quality for legacy O&G wells in CO2 storage assessment areas; supports cement_barrier P10_MTTF=50yr floor for wells with confirmed good CBL; standard-tier detection_prob=0.25 reflects tool sensitivity ceiling not cement absence; wells with bond index <0.5 or partial cement above isolation zone should use minimal tier

reference: IEAGHG 2018-08 §5.3 — Sleipner CO2 storage project: solid particle displacement at high injection rates NOT supported by evidence; halite (NaCl) precipitation from water vaporisation by dry supercritical CO2 is the confirmed dominant near-wellbore injectivity impairment mechanism
notes: Narrows injectivity failure model: halite plugging is the primary P10 scenario; high injection rates exacerbate near-wellbore halite accumulation; injectivity standard-tier detection_prob=0.50 appropriate for halite (detectable via wellhead pressure monitoring and injectivity decline); brine slug injection effective remediation; validates P10_MTTF=8yr for injectivity as capturing halite accumulation timescale

reference: IEAGHG 2018-08 §8.5 — Sminchak et al. 2013: SCP observed in 1940s-era Midwest wells confirmed via gas isotope analysis to NOT originate from the deep gas reservoir first penetrated >70 years prior — demonstrating primary zonal isolation maintained for more than 70 years
notes: Independent field evidence for long-term cement barrier integrity in legacy wells; supports P10_MTTF=50yr floor as conservative for wells with confirmed good cement; SCP may originate from intermediate shallow zones without compromising deep reservoir isolation; complements SACROC 30-year evidence (row 46) with a 70-year longevity data point; shallow gas migration ≠ deep reservoir connectivity

reference: IEAGHG 2018-08 Table 3 — GAO 2014: US EPA Class II CO2 EOR significant noncompliance rate 2008-2012: Texas (Permian Basin) 5-11%; all other states <1%; highest noncompliance in jurisdiction with highest concentration of active CO2 EOR wells
notes: Population-level operational non-conformance benchmark; high_corrosion scenario (failure_prob_multiplier=1.8) appropriate for Permian Basin analogue; base_case represents well-managed lower-noncompliance jurisdictions; note: noncompliance = regulatory/reporting failures not necessarily mechanical failures — directionally consistent with higher failure rates but not directly convertible to MTTF

reference: Physical constraint: hydraulic control line is strapped to outside of production tubing string and routed through the tubing hanger — the line runs in the tubing-casing annulus and cannot be accessed or replaced without pulling the tubing string; wireline TRSV lock-open is a temporary workaround that bypasses rather than repairs the control line
notes: Corrected from rigless_intervention to full_workover; barrier_class corrected from production to safety (control line is the TRSV actuation pathway — loss of control line = loss of ability to close downhole safety barrier); safety_critical and can_defer also corrected accordingly; post_workover_verification_cost applies after every control-line replacement workover per §3.1.2

reference: SPE-170243-MS (Conduit and Pumped Fiber-Optic Systems for Permanent Downhole Monitoring): two architectures — (1) conduit system: fiber runs inside a permanently installed small-bore tube strapped to tubing; fiber retrievable on wireline but conduit requires workover to replace; (2) FIPA pumped fiber: dual-conduit turnaround loop permanently installed; turnaround sub at bottom connects the two conduits; fiber hydraulically pumped down one conduit and returned up the other; fiber sensing element pumped out and replaced at surface with no rig and no tubing pull (truly rigless); the conduit loop itself is permanent and requires a workover if it fails — FIPA separates fiber failure (rigless) from conduit failure (full workover)
notes: Default full_workover models the most conservative case (conventional strapped permanent installation or conduit/loop failure in FIPA systems); operators using FIPA should override intervention_type to rigless_intervention for the fiber sensing element failure mode; conduit failure in FIPA is a separate lower-frequency full_workover event not currently modelled as a distinct component; can_defer=true retained regardless of architecture since monitoring loss is not an emergency trigger

reference: Physical constraint: primary tubing hanger seal (metal-to-metal in a CCS well) sits at the interface between the tubing string and the wellhead bore — primary seal failure requires pulling the tubing string to access and replace the seal assembly; secondary seal re-energisation via wellhead tooling is a temporary workaround not a repair of the primary barrier; analogous to control_line logic
notes: Corrected from light_intervention to full_workover; light intervention only valid for secondary seal activation (workaround); restoring primary barrier integrity requires full tubing pull; safety_critical=true and can_defer=false retained — tubing hanger primary seal failure is a safety-barrier event requiring immediate response

reference: OREDA Handbook (all editions) — data collected from offshore HC production and injection wells in North Sea / Gulf of Mexico / SE Asia
notes: CRITICAL LIMITATION: OREDA covers hydrocarbon service only. CO2 injection is a fundamentally different service environment on three dimensions: (1) Corrosion — supercritical CO2 + formation brine = carbonic acid; HC service corrosion rates do not translate directly; (2) Elastomer compatibility — Nitrile/HNBR degrade in CO2; Aflas/PEEK required; (3) Thermal cycling — CO2 injection temperature vs reservoir temperature creates fatigue cycles absent in HC production. Where OREDA analogues are used in this register they represent the best available starting point only — they should be progressively replaced by CCS-specific field observations as the industry matures. No equivalent of OREDA exists for CCS wells as of 2026.

reference: Field calibration engine — FIELD_C (Quest analogue; Canada; 4 injectors; 9 years of operation): 2 tubing failures observed vs 1.0 expected from P10_MTTF=35yr assumption
notes: First field calibration data point confirming tubing MTTF assumption is optimistic; observed failure rate is 2× model expectation at 10% confidence (2 events); effective calibration factor = 1.10 (10% MTTF reduction) at current confidence level; as more events accumulate confidence will increase and calibration factor influence will grow; this is the expected outcome when transferring HC-service OREDA analogues to CO2 injection service without elastomer/corrosion adjustment
